subject: mathematics
chapter_title: The Mathematics of Maybe: Introduction to Probability

sections.title: What is Probability?
sections.think_reflect: Considers why a coin toss before a cricket match is regarded as a fair way to decide which team bats first.

sections.title: What is Randomness?
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to predict the outcome of a coin toss made by a friend and notice that the result cannot be known in advance even though all outcomes are known.

sections.title: Experimental Probability: Performing Observations or Experiments
Analysing Statistical Data Using Probability
sections.think_reflect: Notes that rolling a 4 eight times in a row does not change the probability of rolling a 4 again, since probability describes long-run behaviour rather than predicting the next outcome.

sections.title: Elements of Probability: Sample Spaces and Events
Sample Space
sections.think_reflect: Considers how the sample space for rainfall must be expanded from {Rain, No Rain} to a finer set such as {No Rain, Drizzle, Light Rain, Heavy Rain} when more detail is required.

sections.title: Tree Diagrams
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to calculate the probability of getting one head and one tail when a fair coin is tossed twice, using the tree diagram already constructed.

prose_summary: Section 7.1 introduces probability as a measurement of likelihood, analogous to measuring length or volume, but applied to events rather than physical quantities; it distinguishes random events (where possible outcomes are known but the actual outcome cannot be predicted) from events with a fixed answer, and introduces subjective probability as an informal, evidence-based judgement of likelihood before any formal measurement is developed. Section 7.1.1 defines randomness and the random experiment, explaining through coin-toss and die-roll examples that an experiment is called random when every repetition can produce a different, unpredictable result even though the full set of possible outcomes is known in advance; it also explains, through the example of rainfall, why an event is considered random when it depends on many complex, sensitive factors that make exact prediction impossible, while still allowing likelihood to be estimated from patterns in data. Section 7.1.2 introduces the probability scale running from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), explains intermediate values such as 0.75 and 0.5 in terms of percentage chance and relative likelihood (more likely, equally likely), and uses a worked deck-of-cards illustration together with a labelled scale (impossible, less likely, even chance, more likely, certain) and a table of everyday events to anchor each band of the scale to a concrete example. Section 7.2 sets out the two routes to an objective probability estimate: experimental probability, obtained by repeating a trial or analysing past data and computing relative frequency, and theoretical probability, obtained by reasoning about equally likely outcomes without running any experiment. Section 7.2.1 defines the experiment, outcome, and sample space, shows how a sample space is written as a bracketed, comma-separated list using coin-toss and die-roll examples, and introduces the experimental probability formula (number of times the event occurred over total number of trials), working through a die-rolling example to compute and interpret a relative frequency. Section 7.2.2 defines theoretical probability as reasoning under the assumption of equally likely outcomes, introduces the P(Event) notation and the formula (favourable outcomes over possible outcomes), and works two examples — the probability of rolling a 4 on a die, and the probability of drawing the letter B from the word PROBABILITY — each solved by explicitly counting favourable and total outcomes. Section 7.2.3 extends probability to statistical data collected from samples, working through a class-survey example on favourite fruit to compute a sample probability and then using that probability to project an estimate onto a larger population (extrapolating from 50 sampled students to 1500 students), introduces the vocabulary of population and sample, and closes by contrasting experimental and theoretical probability, naming the Law of Large Numbers (experimental probability approaches theoretical probability as trials increase) and explicitly correcting the Gambler's Fallacy through a Snakes-and-Ladders illustration showing that each die roll is an independent event unaffected by prior rolls, alongside a definition of a fair, unbiased coin and a random, unbiased toss. Section 7.3 introduces sample spaces and events as the building blocks for organising probability calculations. Section 7.3.1 defines the sample space S as the complete, non-repeating list of possible outcomes, defines sample size n(S), and gives five worked examples of sample spaces of increasing complexity, from a binary rain/no-rain space to the four-outcome space for tossing two coins, the last illustrated with an outcome table. Section 7.3.2 defines an event as a subset of the sample space — any single outcome or combination of outcomes selected from all those that could occur — and works three examples (two coin tosses, a die roll, picking fruit) each showing the full sample space alongside the specific event subset. Section 7.4 introduces tree diagrams as a visual method for enumerating the outcomes of a multi-step experiment built from a sequence of independent trials, explains how branches are drawn stage by stage from a single starting point, and constructs the tree diagram for two coin tosses in full, showing all four branches, labelling each branch with its theoretical probability, deriving the sample space {HH, HT, TH, TT} from the diagram, and computing the probability of the HH outcome (1/4) directly by reading two favourable-outcome counts off the tree.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: States the sample space for tossing a coin once (S = {H, T}) and for rolling a die once (S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}), introducing bracketed sample-space notation.

Given a die rolled 50 times landing on 4 exactly 8 times, computes the experimental probability of rolling a 4 as 8/50 = 0.16 (16%), and identifies this value as the relative frequency of rolling a 4.
Computes the theoretical probability of rolling a 4 on a standard 6-sided die as 1/6 ≈ 0.167 (16.7%), by counting 1 favourable outcome out of 6 possible outcomes.
Computes the probability of picking the letter B at random from the word PROBABILITY as 2/11 ≈ 0.182 (18.2%), counting the 2 occurrences of B among the 11 letters.

From a class survey of 50 students' favourite fruit (20 mango, 15 apple, 10 banana, 5 grapes), computes the probability that a randomly chosen student likes mango as 20/50 = 0.4 (40%), then uses this sample probability to estimate that approximately 600 of 1500 students in the whole school would prefer mango, introducing the terms population and sample.

Using a Snakes and Ladders die-rolling scenario where three consecutive 6s have been rolled, explains why believing a 6 is now less likely on the next roll is the Gambler's Fallacy, since each roll is an independent event with probability 1/6 ≈ 16.6% regardless of past rolls.

Constructs the full tree diagram for tossing a fair coin twice, showing branches for both tosses labelled with probability 1/2 each, derives the sample space S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}, and computes the probability of the HH outcome as 1/4 = 0.25 (25%).

enumerated_exercises.description: Rank four events (Monday following Sunday; snow in Mumbai in July; an elephant walking through the classroom; greeting at least one friend at school tomorrow) on the 0-to-1 probability scale, labelling each as impossible, less likely, equally likely, more likely, or certain, with reasons.

Given a sample of 30 sweets (10 red, 8 green, 7 yellow, 5 blue), (i) calculate the probability that a randomly picked sweet is green, and (ii) estimate, from the sample, how many of 600 sweets in the full bag are likely to be yellow.

Given a survey of 40 students on favourite club (14 Science, 11 Arts, 9 Sports, 6 Debate) out of a school of 800, (i) find the probability a sampled student prefers the Arts Club, and (ii) estimate how many of the 800 students prefer the Sports Club.
Toss a coin 20 times and record heads/tails; (i)-(ii) tally counts, (iii) calculate the experimental probability of heads, (iv) state the probability of tails on one more toss.
Toss a paper cup 100 times, recording whether it lands on its bottom, top, or side each time, and assign experimental probabilities to the three outcomes.
Find the probability of getting an even number when rolling a fair 6-sided die.

Given a 6-sided die rolled 12 times producing a '3' three times, (i) find the experimental probability of rolling a 3, (ii) find the theoretical probability of rolling a 3, (iii) explain why the two might differ and predict what happens as the number of rolls increases to 60, 600, or 6000.
State the total number of possible outcomes in the sample space when a single 6-sided die is rolled.

Write the sample space S for three experiments: (i) rolling a die and tossing a coin together, (ii) choosing a random integer between -5 and +5, (iii) drawing one ball at random from a box of 5 green and 7 red balls.
At a village fair with 3 snacks (Samosa, Pakora, Bhaji) and 2 drinks (Chai, Lassi), (i) list the sample space of all snack-drink combinations, and (ii) list the event 'selecting Samosa as a snack'.

Given Basket A (1 apple, 2 oranges) and Basket B (1 banana, 1 mango), with one fruit picked from each basket, (i) draw a tree diagram of all possible fruit pairs, (ii) list the sample space, (iii) find the probability of picking one apple and one banana.

Given a box of 3 red, 4 black, and 2 green pens, with a pen drawn and replaced and then a friend drawing a pen the same way, (i) list the possible outcome pairs and draw a tree diagram, (ii) use the tree diagram to estimate the probability that both people pick the same colour pen.

Fill in the blanks: (i) the probability of an impossible event, (ii) the name for the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment, (iii) the probability of a certain event, (iv) the probability of getting heads on a fair coin toss.
Given a survey of 50 students where 15 like football, fill in whether the term required is frequency or relative frequency, and give its value as a fraction or decimal.

Decide, with explanation, which of five experiments have equally likely outcomes: a car starting or not; a fair coin toss; a fair 6-sided die roll; drawing a marble from a bag of 3 red and 7 blue marbles; the sex of a newborn baby.

Write the sample space and calculate the probability for five scenarios: (i) at least one head in two coin tosses, (ii) drawing an even-numbered card from cards 1-10, (iii) rolling a number greater than 4 on a die, (iv) drawing a non-red ball from a bag of 3 red, 2 blue, 1 green ball, (v) exactly two heads in three coin tosses.
Given a bag of 3 candies (strawberry, lemon, mint), find the probability of picking the strawberry candy.
List, in table form, all combinations of outfits from 2 shirts (red, blue) and 3 types of pants (jeans, khakis, shorts).

Given a tyre-distance table (20 cases under 4000 km, 210 cases 4001-9000 km, 325 cases 9001-14000 km, 445 cases over 14000 km, total 1000), find the probability a randomly chosen tyre lasts (i) less than 4000 km, (ii) between 4000 and 14000 km, (iii) more than 14000 km.
Given letter cards spelling PEACE, find (i) the probability of drawing a P, E, or C, and (ii) the probability of not drawing an E.
